title: Discovering an Empty Gateway ACL During a Security Assessment
situation: A security assessment for a manufacturing client finds a production system's gateway secinfo file is essentially empty, meaning default permissive behavior applies and any host on the internal network could register arbitrary RFC server programs.
troubleshootingSteps: Confirm the current secinfo/reginfo file contents via SMGW and validate the default-permissive behavior is actually in effect.
Inventory every legitimate RFC server program and calling host currently interacting with this gateway to avoid breaking them during remediation.
Draft a restrictive secinfo/reginfo configuration allowlisting only the confirmed legitimate programs and hosts.
Test the draft configuration extensively in a non-production system mirroring the same interfaces.
Roll out the restrictive configuration to production in a staged, monitored window, watching for any unexpected rejection of legitimate traffic.
Formally document the new baseline and add periodic review to prevent future drift back toward permissive defaults.
rootCause: The gateway ACL files were never configured with restrictive entries after initial system setup, leaving default permissive behavior in place for years - a gap entirely invisible to prior security reviews that focused only on PFCG role design.
resolution: Implemented a carefully tested, restrictive secinfo/reginfo configuration allowlisting only confirmed legitimate RFC programs and hosts, following SAP's documented secure-by-default gateway configuration guidance, after staged non-production validation.
preventiveMeasures: Include gateway ACL configuration review as a mandatory item in every SAP security assessment, not only PFCG/role review
Establish a periodic review cycle re-validating gateway ACL configuration hasn't drifted back toward permissive defaults
Document the legitimate interface inventory as a living artifact to speed future ACL changes

title: Legitimate Interface Blocked After Gateway ACL Tightening
situation: Immediately after a scheduled gateway ACL hardening change goes live in production, a decade-old middleware integration interface stops working, generating urgent escalations from the business team relying on it.
troubleshootingSteps: Confirm the failure timing correlates precisely with the ACL hardening change window rather than an unrelated cause.
Review the gateway's rejection/access control log via SMGW to identify the exact blocked program and host.
Confirm this interface's technical requirements against current documentation, or reconstruct them if undocumented.
Add a precisely scoped allowlist entry for this specific legitimate program/host rather than reverting the broader ACL hardening.
Retest the interface end-to-end to confirm restored functionality.
Update the interface inventory to prevent this gap from recurring in future ACL changes.
rootCause: The decade-old middleware interface was undocumented and missing from the pre-hardening interface inventory, so it was not included in the new restrictive gateway ACL allowlist.
resolution: Added a precisely scoped secinfo/reginfo entry for the specific legitimate program and host, restoring the interface without reverting the overall ACL hardening improvement.
preventiveMeasures: Build and maintain a complete, current interface inventory before any future gateway ACL tightening project
Stage ACL changes through non-production validation against all known interfaces before production rollout
Treat undocumented legacy interfaces discovered this way as a signal to formally document them going forward
diagram:
flowchart LR
  A[Gateway ACL Hardening Deployed] --> B{Interface in Pre-Hardening Inventory?}
  B -->|Yes| C[Allowlisted - Continues Working]
  B -->|No| D[Blocked - Legitimate Interface Breaks]
  D --> E[Add Precisely Scoped Allowlist Entry]
  E --> C

title: Unexpected Cross-System Access via an Overly Broad Trusted RFC Relationship
situation: During an incident investigation, a user is found to have performed actions in a production system despite never having any role or authorization assigned there directly, traced back to a trusted RFC relationship with another system.
troubleshootingSteps: Confirm the user's direct role assignments in the affected system show no authorization for the observed actions.
Identify any trusted/trusting RFC relationship configured between this system and other systems in the landscape.
Review the S_RFCACL configuration on the trusting system to determine which calling systems and users are actually permitted to use the trust relationship.
Confirm whether S_RFCACL was configured too broadly, permitting the observed user (originating from the trusted system) to pass through without restriction.
Restrict S_RFCACL to only the specific calling systems and users with a genuine, documented business need for the trust relationship.
Audit for any other trust relationships in the landscape with similarly broad S_RFCACL configuration.
rootCause: The trusted/trusting RFC relationship between the two systems had no restrictive S_RFCACL configuration, allowing any user authenticated in the trusted system to act in the trusting system without holding any direct role or authorization there.
resolution: Scoped S_RFCACL on the trusting system to explicitly restrict which calling system and specific users may use the trust relationship, eliminating the unintended broad pass-through access.
preventiveMeasures: Never configure a trusted/trusting RFC relationship without an explicit, narrowly scoped S_RFCACL restriction
Include trust relationship configuration in standard periodic access reviews, not only direct role assignments
Require documented business justification for every trust relationship at the time of configuration

title: Unauthorized Production Change Traced to a Bypassed Transport Approval Process
situation: An audit discovers a production role change granting broad new access was never reviewed through the standard transport approval process, and investigation reveals it reached production through a transport route that bypassed the normal governance gate.
troubleshootingSteps: Trace the specific transport request ID and confirm its actual approval history against the expected STMS workflow.
Identify which transport route or system landscape configuration allowed this transport to bypass the standard approval gate.
Determine whether this was a one-off misconfiguration or a systemic gap in the transport route/domain configuration.
Review other recent transports for the same bypass pattern to scope the full extent of unauthorized changes.
Correct the transport route/domain configuration to close the bypass and require all transports to pass through the standard approval gate.
Reverse or formally re-review the unauthorized role change through the correct approval process retroactively.
rootCause: A transport route misconfiguration allowed a specific import path to bypass the standard STMS approval workflow, permitting a role change with broad new access to reach production without any review, including no security-specific check of the authorization-relevant content.
resolution: Corrected the transport route configuration to eliminate the bypass path, retroactively reviewed the unauthorized change through the proper approval process, and reverted the broadened access pending formal justification.
preventiveMeasures: Periodically audit transport route/domain configuration for unintended bypass paths, not only the approval workflow itself
Implement automated detection flagging any transport containing authorization-relevant objects (roles, SU24, DCL) for mandatory security review
Treat transport governance as a security control requiring the same audit rigor as authorization design itself

title: One Protocol Left Unprotected After a Web Dispatcher-Only Hardening Effort
situation: A landscape recently completed a thorough Web Dispatcher hardening project including HTTPS enforcement and URL filtering, yet a subsequent penetration test still finds significant exposure through classic SAP GUI/RFC access.
troubleshootingSteps: Confirm the penetration test's findings specifically relate to classic RFC/DIAG protocol access rather than any HTTP(S)-based path already addressed.
Review the current SAProuter configuration and routing permission table (saprouttab) for restrictiveness.
Compare the level of rigor applied to SAProuter hardening against what was applied to Web Dispatcher during the recent project.
Identify the specific gap - typically an overly permissive or default saprouttab entry left unaddressed.
Draft and test a restrictive SAProuter routing configuration in non-production before applying to production.
Roll out the corrected SAProuter configuration and re-run the penetration test to confirm the gap is closed.
rootCause: The recent hardening project scoped its work exclusively to Web Dispatcher and HTTP(S) traffic, leaving SAProuter's classic RFC/DIAG protocol configuration at its prior, more permissive state - the two components protect entirely different protocols, so hardening one provided no protection for the other.
resolution: Extended the hardening effort to SAProuter, tightening the routing permission table to restrict classic RFC/DIAG access to only legitimate required paths, closing the protocol gap the penetration test identified.
preventiveMeasures: Explicitly scope any perimeter hardening project to cover both Web Dispatcher (HTTP/HTTPS) and SAProuter (RFC/DIAG) as distinct, mandatory workstreams
Include both protocols in the acceptance criteria and penetration test scope before declaring a hardening project complete
Document the protocol-specific responsibility split clearly so future projects don't repeat the single-protocol scoping mistake
diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[Hardening Project Scope] --> B[Web Dispatcher - HTTP/HTTPS]
  A -.->|Not Included| C[SAProuter - RFC/DIAG]
  B --> D[HTTP Path Secured]
  C --> E[RFC/DIAG Path Still Exposed]
  E --> F[Penetration Test Finding]
  F --> G[Extend Hardening to SAProuter]
